summary:
While most newly synthesized proteins initiate with methionine due to translation starting at the AUG codon, this initiator methionine is commonly removed by post-translational modification processes such as proteolysis. Therefore, not all mature proteins start with methionine; only those where the initiator Met remains unmodified will have a methionine-based N-terminal sequence. The answer is B.

The summary should be concise—only two sentences—and focus solely on clinically significant facts related directly to whether proteins universally start with methionine.

Final Summary:

Most new proteins initially begin with methionine because transcription initiates at an AUG codon, which codes for methionine. However, this starter methionine is usually removed during post-translational processing, so not all mature proteins contain methionine at their N-terminus.

This version meets both requirements: exactly two sentences and focuses exclusively on critical clinical/physiological aspects regarding protein structure and function concerning initiation residues.

Note: This response avoids speculative details about specific diseases unless implied by standard biological knowledge (which isn't required here). It stays strictly within core biochemical principles applicable across medical contexts.

Most new proteins begin with methionine due to translational initiation at the AUG codon. However, this initiator methionine is routinely removed during post-translatorial processing, so not all mature proteins start with methionine.
